summary: Graham argues that describing Y Combinator as simply a seed fund misses the point because at the earliest stage, money matters far less than hands-on help. YC fills a new niche created by cheaper web startups, acting like a launch system that gets fragile companies airborne and better prepared for later funding. This makes YC a different kind of venture “animal,” not a smaller version of traditional VCs.
key_points: At the earliest stage, startups need help and direction more than capital, so YC optimizes for guidance and setup rather than money.
YC is a distinct business model and niche from VC, enabled by dramatically lower startup costs in software.
Hands-on, concentrated support over a short period can materially increase a startup’s trajectory and valuation.
The accelerator model creates new deal flow by pushing would-be employees to found companies.
money_quotes: Saying YC does seed funding for startups is a description in terms of earlier models. It's like calling a car a horseless carriage.
At our end, money is almost a negligible factor.
What we really do at Y Combinator is get startups launched straight.
Reward is always proportionate to risk, and very early stage startups are insanely risky.
Really we're more of a small, furry steam catapult.
key_insight: YC’s core value isn’t capital but concentrated early-stage guidance that gets fragile startups airborne in a new, cheaper-to-build ecosystem.
